landscape.title: Market Landscape — Moore's 8-stage lifecycle
landscape.subtitle: Where the 145 firms sit on Moore's 8-stage market lifecycle. Hype cycle overlay (rising/falling) + tier classification (gorilla/potential, etc.) combined into one integrated curve.
landscape.auxLabelNote: 💡 Notation — Moore's book defines 6 tiers (Gorilla/Chimpanzee/Monkey + King/Prince/Serf). 'In Chasm' is not a tier but a **position/state** (any category can fall into the chasm and return to its own tier when it exits). 'Potential Gorilla' is our club's **auxiliary label** for marking pre-tornado candidates. Both are operational tools, not the main thesis.

ecosystems.title: Ecosystems × Value-Chain Layers
ecosystems.subtitle: Map each firm into one of 9 macro themes and locate it in a specific value-chain layer. Moore's whole-product principle — one gorilla per category — is auto-validated within each layer.
ecosystems.totalSummary: 9 ecosystems · 51 layers · {primary} primary + {secondary} secondary cross-cut + {uncategorized} uncategorized
ecosystems.uncategorizedHint: Financial · Telecom · Consumer Staples — candidates for future ecosystem expansion
ecosystems.notMapped: Not yet mapped to any ecosystem. Will be added after classification discussion.
ecosystems.momentumExplain: Price = mean of Yahoo Finance 1M change (live data) · News = headline sentiment average (currently mock + keyword heuristic)
ecosystems.overlaySubtitle: All series rebased to 100 at 12W start · click to toggle
ecosystems.firmCategoryHint: Even within the same layer, this firm holds different positions (Gorilla/Prince/Challenger) per category. With category lifecycle phase, this is the primary input for buy/sell/rebalance signals.
ecosystems.noCategoriesInLayer: Categories not yet defined for this layer (may only have secondary firms).
ecosystems.firmSellPanelHint: Auto-generated from category phase + firm role + price momentum + news sentiment + Moore conflict. Tier unchanged — use as discussion trigger.
ecosystems.funnelHint: All candidates → potential → price-validated → long-term hold. 145 firms auto-classified into 4 stages.
ecosystems.sentimentDrivenHint: Weighted: price volatility + news sentiment + hype cycle + narrative valuation. Tier unchanged — use to weigh buy/sell with extra caution.
ecosystems.firmSentimentPanelHint: 4 factors: price volatility · news sentiment · hype cycle · narrative valuation. Higher score = price moves more on sentiment than fundamentals.
ecosystems.substitutionHint: Where revenue is migrating from declining to new categories. Whether incumbents play in the successor is the key to buy/sell judgement.
ecosystems.listSectionHint: 145 firms classified into Moore × value-chain matrix. Click a card for that ecosystem's layer × phase matrix.
ecosystems.uncategorizedSectionHint: Financial · Telecom · Consumer Staples — candidates for future ecosystem expansion.

footer.disclaimer: Based on Geoffrey Moore's trilogy and Miller Heiman's The New Strategic Selling · Mock data only · Not financial advice

dashboard.title: Gorilla Game Dashboard
dashboard.subtitle: Track US tech companies through Geoffrey Moore's framework from Crossing the Chasm, Inside the Tornado, and The Gorilla Game. Gorillas and Potential Gorillas are your buy signals.
dashboard.whyGorillaBody: Geoffrey Moore's framework identifies that in every technology category that undergoes a Tornado — a period of explosive mass-market adoption — one company emerges as the Gorilla: the firm whose proprietary architecture becomes the de facto standard. Gorillas compound wealth because their switching costs make customers nearly impossible to lose. The strategy: buy gorillas and potential gorillas during or just before the tornado, and hold.
dashboard.sectionMarketTitle: Where is the market right now?
dashboard.sectionMarketHint: Where the 145 firms cluster on Moore's 8-stage market lifecycle. Category transitions (declining → emerging) are also shown.
dashboard.sectionBuyHint: Gorilla / Potential Gorilla tiers are buy candidates. Once price validates, they move to long-term hold (Investment Funnel).
dashboard.sectionSellHint: Auto-signals from category decline · late main street · sharp news / Moore conflicts. Tier unchanged — use as discussion triggers.
dashboard.sectionFlowHint: Layer-level acceleration / deceleration from live Yahoo prices, plus firms with the largest fundamentals-vs-sentiment gap.
dashboard.sectionLearnHint: Drill into the ecosystem matrix, search firms, study theory, or join discussions.

firms.title: All Firms
firms.subtitle: 50 US tech companies ranked by Gorilla Game score. Click any row for full analysis.
firms.dataAsOf: Data as of {date} · Prices & market cap from Yahoo Finance (live), classification scores from rules-based engine
firms.scoringThesis: Each company is scored 0–100 across 7 dimensions, and the weighted total determines how close it is to gorilla status. Score alone doesn't decide classification — architecture control, switching costs, and market share must each pass critical thresholds.
firms.thPotential: Potential Gorilla — arch≥60, switching or network≥50
firms.thresholdsNote: Architecture control score determines the track. Arch≥40 → proprietary market (left), below → open market (right).
firms.searchPlaceholder: Search by name, ticker, or sector...

firmDetail.noNews: No news articles for this firm yet.
firmDetail.revenueNoData: No revenue segment data available yet.

scoreDescriptions.marketShare: Estimated share of the firm's specific technology niche
scoreDescriptions.switchingCosts: How hard it is for customers to leave (NRR, proprietary protocols)
scoreDescriptions.architectureControl: Whether the firm defines the standard others must conform to
scoreDescriptions.marketConcentration: Whether the market is consolidating around this firm
scoreDescriptions.networkEffects: Strength of increasing returns — more users/developers create exponentially more platform value
scoreDescriptions.ecosystemControl: Degree to which partners, developers, and customers build their business on this firm's platform

learn.title: The Gorilla Game Framework
learn.thesis: In disruptive technology markets, one company seizes the proprietary architecture, locks customers in through switching costs, and subjugates competitors via network effects. That company is the gorilla. Finding the gorilla before the tornado, buying it, and holding until a proven substitution threat emerges — that is the entire gorilla game.
learn.subtitle: This page contains the complete theoretical framework extracted from five books, arranged in the causal sequence of 'why does exactly one company win.' Follow the flow below to learn how to identify gorillas yourself.
learn.whySubtitle: There are countless investing methodologies — value, momentum, theme. Why the Gorilla Game? Chapter 1 of the book answers directly.
learn.whyThesis: “To give the risk-averse, capital-constrained investor access to the high returns of high-tech stocks while sidestepping the brutal volatility of that market.” That is the true purpose of the Gorilla Game. (Ch. 1, p. 50)
learn.whyContext: Life expectancy is rising while social safety nets thin out. Society is shifting from institutional trust toward self-reliance — families must fund their own retirements, which means returns must beat traditional investment vehicles by a wide margin. But that doesn't mean exposing the family savings to the brutal volatility of high-tech markets. This dilemma is the reason the Gorilla Game exists.

upside.title: Upside is still enormous
upside.desc: Microsoft's market cap was about $660M at its 1986 IPO and exceeded $130B ten years later — a 200× return. Not 2×, not 10×, but 200×. The next 50× compounder is still out there to be caught.

downside.title: Downside is small
downside.desc: The Gorilla Game does not bet as the cards fall. You only buy when a category market has entered hyper-growth, and during your holding period stocks in such markets generally do not lose money.

tolerantOfMistakes.title: Mistakes are forgiven
tolerantOfMistakes.desc: The game does not demand precise timing or a perfect basket — only that the basket contains the eventual gorilla. It is, as the book puts it, “an honest game for honest people.”

learn.whyConstraintsLabel: But, the 3 Constraints of This Game

discipline.title: Strict discipline is required
discipline.desc: If you love trading frequently, if gambling, gut-feel, or contrarian instincts pull at you — this is not your game. The Gorilla Game requires the discipline to grab the obvious winner and never let go.

research.title: You must track the high-tech industry
research.desc: You don't have to be an expert. But you do have to follow what's happening in the high-tech industry — set aside time, away from family, to study. You only need to follow one category at a time; you don't need to play many gorilla games at once.

fairFunds.title: Use the funds with restraint
fairFunds.desc: This is not a game for grabbing as much money as possible. If that's your goal, look elsewhere. The Gorilla Game offers a sensible way to invest the family savings — nothing more, nothing less.

learn.whyAlternativesTitle: How It Differs From Other Methodologies
learn.whyAlternativesSubtitle: The Gorilla Game is a form of growth investing. But Chapter 1 directly contrasts it with five common alternatives — and the differences are decisive.

concept.name: Concept Investing vs Gorilla Game
concept.vs: Vague speculation → Proven financial track record
concept.desc: Concept investing buys on hype right after the IPO; those success stories are usually short-lived. The Gorilla Game enters only after the market is well developed, anchoring on real revenue. It does not get caught in false hyper-growth.

momentum.name: Momentum Investing vs Gorilla Game
momentum.vs: Buy the whole category → Buy the single safest name in it
momentum.desc: Momentum investing buys every name in the category — “follow the herd until everyone falls off the cliff.” The Gorilla Game picks only the single safest play in that category (the gorilla). It is the one safe ride on the same trend.

theme.name: Theme Investing vs Gorilla Game
theme.vs: Broad portfolio → Consolidate into one name
theme.desc: Theme investing buys a dozen names tied to a macro trend. The Gorilla Game starts from a small basket and consolidates into a single position — the gorilla. That's why it can succeed with limited capital and limited industry knowledge.

value.name: Value Investing vs Gorilla Game
value.vs: Hunt the underpriced → Hunt the obvious champion
value.desc: Value investors look for unloved, underpriced stocks. Gorilla stocks are never that. Microsoft was never a value stock. The two portfolios share almost no names.

technical.name: Technical Analysis vs Gorilla Game
technical.vs: Follow the price → Follow the industry dynamics
technical.desc: Technical analysis only looks at the chart. It ignores industry dynamics. The Gorilla Game runs in the opposite direction — it starts from market structure, competitive advantage, and category dynamics.

learn.whyEssence: Bottom line: the Gorilla Game is a form of growth investing, with two distinguishing features — (1) it focuses exclusively on product-oriented companies in high-tech hyper-growth markets, and (2) it uses concentration, not diversification, as its risk-reduction strategy. Those two choices are what separate it from every other methodology. (Ch. 1, p. 59)
learn.landscapeTitle: ⓪-2 The Gorilla Game on the Map of Investing Methodologies
learn.landscapeSubtitle: The five comparisons in Chapter 1 (concept/momentum/theme/value/technical) reflect the book's 1999 perspective. Retail investors today encounter five more methodologies in everyday life — index, dividend, GARP, quality, DCA. This section places the Gorilla Game on that broader map and explains why it fits ordinary investors.
learn.landscapeMapLabel: Beyond the book — 5 methodologies retail investors actually encounter (web-sourced)

index.name: Index · Passive Investing
index.desc: Bogle launched the Vanguard S&P 500 fund in 1976 (now ≈ $1.5T AUM). From July 2024–June 2025, only 33% of active funds beat their passive counterparts; over the long term, 75%+ of active funds trail the index. That's why it became the retail default. Diversifies away single-stock risk — but is structurally locked at the market average.

dividend.name: Dividend Investing
dividend.desc: Quarterly dividends and lower volatility from mature firms — popular with retirees. Drawbacks are clear: (1) dividends aren't guaranteed and get cut in crises, (2) less retained capital limits capital gains, (3) double taxation (corporate + personal), (4) ‘dividend traps’ where falling prices inflate yield.

garp.name: GARP — Growth at a Reasonable Price
garp.desc: Peter Lynch's approach. Hunt companies growing earnings 15–25% annually with a PEG ratio (P/E ÷ growth) at or below 1.0. Lynch returned ~29% per year at Magellan (1977–1990), growing the fund from $18M to $14B. ‘Invest in what you know’ — companies you spot in everyday life count.
garp.fit: ✅ Retail investors who can read financials

quality.name: Quality · Wide Moat Investing
quality.desc: Buffett & Munger's approach. ‘Better to buy a wonderful company at a fair price than a fair company at a wonderful price.’ Verify the moat — brand (Coca-Cola), switching costs, network effects, scale — then concentrate and hold long. Berkshire has compounded ~20% per year since 1965.
quality.fit: ✅ The closest cousin to the Gorilla Game

dca.name: DCA — Dollar Cost Averaging
dca.vs: Buy a fixed amount on a schedule
dca.desc: Don't lump-sum; invest the same amount monthly. Vanguard's 1926–2011 backtest shows lump-sum mathematically beats DCA 67% of the time (avg. 2–3% over 10y). But behaviorally, DCA softens volatility-fear and helps investors actually stay invested. ‘Investors live in sequences, not averages.’
dca.fit: ✅ A buying schedule — combinable with any strategy

learn.landscapePositionSubtitle: On the standard 5-axis taxonomy of investment strategies, here is exactly where the Gorilla Game sits.

horizon.label: Time horizon
horizon.note: Don't sell until a substitution threat is proven.

portfolio.label: Portfolio strategy
portfolio.note: Risk is reduced by consolidation, not diversification.

efficiency.label: Market assumption
efficiency.note: The market underprices the long-run impact of discontinuous innovation.

style.label: Style
style.note: Growth, but restricted to high-tech hyper-growth markets.

learn.landscapePositionEssence: One-line summary — within high-tech categories, pick a single name through fundamental industry-structure analysis, hold indefinitely, and assume the market is structurally inefficient.
learn.landscapeRetailLabel: So — why this game fits retail investors (4 real-world constraints)
learn.landscapeRetailSubtitle: Compare other methodologies against the four real-world constraints faced by ‘risk-averse, capital-constrained’ retail investors. The Gorilla Game's fit becomes obvious.

lowCapital.title: Low capital → concentration beats diversification
lowCapital.desc: $10,000 across 30 names = $333 each. Trading costs and tracking burden are inefficient. Index ETFs solve this — but lock you to the market average. The Gorilla Game concentrates capital efficiently in a single category champion. Same philosophy as Buffett & Munger: ‘bet big when you have real conviction.’

limitedTime.title: Time-poor → indefinite holds = fewer decisions
limitedTime.desc: GARP and quality investing still require quarterly earnings analysis. The Gorilla Game says: ‘once you grab the obvious winner, don't sell until a substitution threat is proven’ — perhaps 5–10 trade decisions in a lifetime. The most realistic strategy for someone with a day job.

limitedExpertise.title: Limited expertise → one industry pattern, not accounting
limitedExpertise.desc: Value investing demands DCF and residual-income models. Technical analysis demands chart-pattern memorization. The Gorilla Game asks one question: ‘who captured the architecture standard?’ — an industry pattern, not a finance certification. You need time to track the industry, not the skills of an accountant.

emotionalRisk.title: Emotional risk → frequent trading is blocked by design
emotionalRisk.desc: Momentum trading is a ‘exit-just-before-the-fall’ timing game — virtually impossible for retail emotions (you fall off the cliff with the herd). The Gorilla Game's ‘hold indefinitely’ rule structurally blocks loss-aversion, confirmation bias, and herd behavior.

learn.landscapeCombo: For most retail investors, an index fund is the default — it solves the time/knowledge gap with the market average. But if you have the will and interest to track high-tech, you can carve out a portion of your portfolio for the Gorilla Game and aim for alpha. The two strategies share the same core philosophy (long-term holds · no market timing · self-reliance), so they complement rather than conflict.
learn.audienceTitle: ⓪-3 Who Should Play This Game
learn.audienceSubtitle: In the introduction, the three authors define their reader very precisely. If you fit this picture — this game is for you.

primary.title: The individual investor saving for the family
primary.desc: Not wealthy. Starts with limited capital. Worries constantly about losing it. “Risk-averse and capital-constrained” — this is the book's true reader.

knowledge.title: Someone with mid-tier knowledge
knowledge.desc: More industry knowledge than a retail broker but less than a venture capitalist. More investing knowledge than a business reporter but less than a professional fund manager. Too little or too much knowledge erodes the book's value.

goal.title: Conservative but ambitious
goal.desc: Wants to build wealth faster and larger than traditional vehicles allow — yet hates risk. Wants to ride high-tech upside while protecting the family savings. The book is written precisely for that dilemma.

exec.title: High-tech executives
exec.desc: Get a consistent analytical framework for discussing how business strategy affects the stock — a shared vocabulary for talking to Wall Street, employees, and customers.

vc.title: Venture capital partners
vc.desc: In a sense, VCs are excellent gorilla-game operators — they run the game from the very beginning of the technology adoption life cycle. The book's scenarios sharpen post-IPO ‘liquidity event’ decisions.

fund.title: Tech fund managers
fund.desc: Get insight that links manager behavior, market reaction, market position, and stock price causally. Some intentionally relax the book's conservative rules to chase higher returns.

learn.audienceMisfitLabel: Who this book is NOT for

audienceMisfit.title: Day traders
audienceMisfit.desc: “Day trading is closer to gambling than investing.” Unless you can break the short-term trading habit, the goals and methods in this book have nothing to do with you. Day trading only works in a bull market — when everyone is making money anyway.

learn.talcSubtitle: Every disruptive technology market follows these six stages. Investment opportunity is concentrated in a single window — the Tornado.
learn.hypeSubtitle: If TALC shows 'who is buying,' the Hype Cycle shows 'where market expectations are.' Reading both curves together reveals the investment timing.
learn.hypeThesis: The Hype Cycle's 'Trough of Disillusionment' roughly coincides with TALC's Chasm. When expectations hit bottom and the Slope of Enlightenment begins — that's the Bowling Alley → Tornado entry signal. You must track both market hype and actual adoption (TALC) simultaneously to capture the buy window.

hype.troughDesc: After the peak of inflated expectations, disappointment floods in. The press declares the technology 'dead.' But this moment is precisely the Chasm — pragmatists simply haven't bought yet. Right when public attention vanishes, the company that secured a beachhead quietly enters the Bowling Alley.
hype.insightDesc: If the Hype Cycle shows entry into the 'Slope of Enlightenment' while TALC signals a Bowling Alley → Tornado transition — that's the gorilla candidate buy window. Conversely, if hype is at its peak but TALC is still in Early Market, a Chasm crash awaits. Always cross-validate the two indicators.

learn.chasmTitle: ② The Chasm — Where Most Companies Die
learn.chasmThesis: Between early adopters (visionaries) and the early majority (pragmatists) lies an unbridgeable gap. Visionary references don't work for pragmatists. If a technology fails to cross this gap, it dies in the market — no matter how brilliant.

whyDie.title: Why Most Die Here
whyDie.desc: Visionaries (early adopters) buy incomplete technology for competitive advantage. But pragmatists (early majority) only buy proven solutions. A visionary's success story is not a valid reference for a pragmatist — they live in different worlds. This disconnect is the chasm.

wholeProduct.title: The Whole Product
wholeProduct.desc: Pragmatists don't buy technology alone. They want the complete package: technology + installation + training + support + integration + standards. To cross the chasm, you must first build a whole product for a single niche market.

beachhead.title: Beachhead Strategy (D-Day Analogy)
beachhead.desc: Like the Normandy invasion, you don't attack the entire market at once. Pick one narrow niche (beachhead), achieve overwhelming victory there with a whole product. That win becomes the reference that pragmatists need to expand into adjacent markets.

learn.tornadoTitle: ③ The Tornado — The Moment the Gorilla Is Born
learn.tornadoThesis: When a technology that crossed the chasm hits critical mass, pragmatists move simultaneously. They pick the 'market leader' — because it's safe. This positive feedback loop concentrates customers, partners, and developers onto one company, whose architecture becomes the de facto standard. This is the birth of the gorilla.

herdBehavior.title: Pragmatist Herd Behavior
herdBehavior.desc: Pragmatists don't decide independently. They watch what their peers buy. They buy 'whatever is selling the most.' This herd behavior explosively concentrates demand onto the market leader.

feedbackLoop.title: Positive Feedback Loop
feedbackLoop.desc: More customers → more partners/developers → richer ecosystem → even more customers. This self-reinforcing cycle makes one company the overwhelming #1. The gap with #2 widens over time.

architectureWar.title: Architecture Standard War
architectureWar.desc: The real battle in the tornado is 'whose architecture becomes the standard.' The company that captures the standard becomes the gorilla — competitors must conform to its interfaces, and customers find it hard to leave (switching costs). Losers become chimps.

winnerTakeAll.title: Winner-Take-All Outcome
winnerTakeAll.desc: After the tornado, market structure solidifies: 1 gorilla (standard owner), 1-2 chimps (niche survivors), many monkeys (low-cost clones). Kings lead markets with no proprietary architecture. This structure doesn't change until the next disruptive innovation.

learn.productLifeCycleTitle: ③-2 Product Life Cycle — The Journey After the Tornado
learn.productLifeCycleSubtitle: TALC takes you through the chasm. Moore's Living on the Fault Line (2000) extends the story: Main Street is actually three sub-phases, followed by the Fault Line and End of Life.
learn.productLifeCycleThesis: Once the Tornado crowns a gorilla, a long Main Street begins. In reality it has three sub-phases — Thriving, Maturing, Declining — before a Fault Line rupture and eventual End of Life. The dashboard's 'Firms by Market Phase' chart classifies all 146 firms against this extended model automatically.

phase1.title: Phase 1 — Crossing the Chasm
phase1.desc: Early Market → Bowling Alley → Tornado. The race to become the standard.

phase2.title: Phase 2 — Main Street
phase2.desc: Thriving · Maturing · Declining Main Street. The biggest revenue and market cap era.

phase3.title: Phase 3 — Decline
phase3.desc: Fault Line · End of Life. Replacement technology fractures the category.

thriving.name: Thriving Main Street
thriving.desc: Right after the Tornado. The gorilla expands fast, switching costs accrue, the ecosystem solidifies.

maturing.name: Maturing Main Street
maturing.desc: The cash-cow era. High margins, free cash flow, dividends — market cap near its peak.

declining.name: Declining Main Street
declining.desc: Growth flattens but still profitable. Watch for next-gen tech or rising competitors.

faultLine.name: Fault Line
faultLine.desc: Moore's 'structural rupture.' A new paradigm threatens the whole category; revenue turns negative and transformation pressure mounts.

learn.productLifeCycleDashboardCallout: 💡 This model drives the dashboard's 'Firms by Market Phase' chart — curve height reflects the revenue/market-cap scale of each stage, and the per-phase cards show the automatic classifications.
learn.anatomyTitle: ④ Anatomy of a Gorilla — 7-Dimension Framework
learn.anatomyThesis: You don't pick gorillas by gut feeling. We identify them through **Moore's 4 core criteria (70%) + 3 auxiliary dimensions our club added (30%)**. The main thesis is limited to Moore's 4 — the extra variables are club analysis tools.
learn.anatomyMooreDesc: The core gorilla-judgment criteria Geoffrey Moore explicitly named in 'The Gorilla Game' (1998). Combined weight 70%.
learn.anatomyAuxDesc: Variables our club added to rank candidates that already passed Moore's 4. Not specified in Moore's books. Combined weight 30%.

architectureControl.title: Architecture Control
architectureControl.desc: Does this company define the technical standard that competitors must follow? This is the gorilla's most essential trait. Like NVIDIA's CUDA or Microsoft's Windows API — whoever owns the standard owns the market.

switchingCosts.title: Switching Costs
switchingCosts.desc: How hard is it for customers to leave? Measured by net revenue retention (NRR), proprietary protocols, and data lock-in. The higher the switching costs, the deeper the gorilla's moat.

marketShare.title: Category Dominance
marketShare.desc: Market share within the specific technology niche. The wider the gap between #1 and #2 in a tornado market, the higher the gorilla probability.

networkEffects.title: Network Effects
networkEffects.desc: Does the platform's value increase exponentially as users and developers grow? This increasing-returns structure compounds the gorilla's competitive advantage over time.

ecosystemControl.title: Ecosystem Control
ecosystemControl.desc: Do partners, ISVs, and developers build their businesses on this company's platform? The thicker the ecosystem, the higher the switching costs and the more entrenched the gorilla position.

revenueGrowth.title: Revenue Growth
revenueGrowth.desc: Year-over-year revenue growth. Gorillas in the tornado stage show explosive growth. However, the 5 structural factors above matter more than growth rate alone for gorilla identification.

marketConcentration.title: Market Concentration
marketConcentration.desc: Is the market consolidating around this company? Look for decreasing competitor count, increasing M&A activity, and market restructuring signals.

earlyMarket.name: Early Market
earlyMarket.desc: Visionaries and tech enthusiasts buy. No mainstream adoption yet. High risk.

chasm.name: Chasm
chasm.desc: The gap between visionaries and pragmatists. Most companies die here. Avoid investing.

bowlingAlley.name: Bowling Alley
bowlingAlley.desc: Niche-by-niche adoption begins. A gorilla candidate is forming. Watch closely.

tornado.name: Tornado
tornado.desc: Explosive mass-market adoption. The gorilla emerges. This is the BUY window.

mainStreet.name: Main Street
mainStreet.desc: Growth normalizes. Gorilla dominates. Hold your position; switching costs protect you.

endOfLife.name: End of Life
endOfLife.desc: New paradigm displacing the old. Exit the gorilla. Find the next tornado.

learn.nonIctTitle: ④-B Non-ICT Industry Cases — Universality of Moore's 4 Criteria
learn.nonIctSubtitle: Moore's 4 criteria operate in industries beyond IT. For each case we examine how the 4 criteria distribute, what tier/position results, and whether the framework still applies — validating that the same analytical lens works on non-ICT categories.

cocaCola.title: Coca-Cola (KO) — 100-year primate gorilla
cocaCola.criteria: Architecture ✅ Brand + secret formula (proprietary IP) · Switching ✅ Consumer habits & taste conditioning · Network ✅ Global distribution (convenience stores, restaurants, vending) · Whole Product ✅ Marketing, licensing, local-bottler infrastructure
cocaCola.insight: All 4 Moore criteria pass. Textbook example of a non-ICT primate gorilla. Market cap ~$280B (2025).

telecom3.title: Motorola → Nokia → Apple — Category PLC + primate-tier replacement
telecom3.criteria: Each era's category (analog/feature-phone/smartphone) had a primate gorilla passing all 4 — standard, OS, apps, telco infra, developer ecosystem.
telecom3.status: PLC peak/decline → new gorilla in next category
telecom3.insight: Each era's primate gorilla still died because the category itself died. PLC + primate jungle operate together — the gap is permanent within a category, but categories themselves don't last.

emartCoupang.title: E-Mart → Coupang — Main Street → Tornado handoff
emartCoupang.criteria: E-Mart: Whole Product ✅ (full-stack weekend family shopping) / Architecture ⚠️ (anyone can open a store). Coupang: Switching↑ (Rocket lock-in) / Network↑ (Coupang Play + marketplace) / Architecture strengthening (proprietary logistics IP).
emartCoupang.status: E-Mart Main Street → Coupang Tornado in progress
emartCoupang.insight: Retail also follows Tornado → Main Street → next Tornado cycles. Progress on the 4 criteria determines who replaces #1.

dyson.title: Dyson — Category-level gorilla (beachhead + bowling alley)
dyson.criteria: Architecture ✅ Motor IP (Digital Motor V11/V12) · Switching ⚠️ Weak (long replacement cycle, no cross-category lock-in) · Network ❌ Almost none · Whole Product ✅ Dedicated accessories/service
dyson.status: #1 in each category (vacuum, hair dryer, air purifier)
dyson.insight: Partial pass on Moore 4. Category-level gorilla, not a company-level primate gorilla — analyze category by category.

kitchen.title: Briquette stove → coal briquette → gas range → induction — PLC + TALC together
kitchen.criteria: Each era's category has its own PLC curve. TALC also runs separately within each category (innovator → early adopter → pragmatist → ...).
kitchen.status: Induction in Tornado now; gas range in Decline
kitchen.insight: The category itself changes within an industry. The #1 of each category becomes a chimp in the next — PLC is the mechanism that cuts off primate-tier dominance.

learn.nonIctClosing: 💡 Pattern — Moore's 4 criteria operate identically in beverage, telecom, retail, and home-appliance industries. However vs IT: (1) #1 gaps are smaller (30~50% share), (2) category PLC may be shorter, breaking primate-tier dominance more easily. Hence the core focus remains the IT primate jungle; non-ICT is a supplementary validation domain.
learn.tiersSubtitle: Moore's book defines **6 tiers (Gorilla/Chimpanzee/Monkey + King/Prince/Serf)**. Our club added **Potential Gorilla and In-Chasm position** as auxiliary labels for operational analysis — not main thesis, just analysis tools.
learn.tiersPrimateDesc: Different DNA — interbreeding impossible. The gap is permanent: once a gorilla, the chimp can never catch up.
learn.tiersKingdomDesc: A status-mobility game within the same species. The throne can be shaken — that's why kings get WATCH (not BUY like a primate gorilla).
learn.tiersAuxDesc: Labels not in Moore's 6 tiers. 'Pot Gorilla' marks pre-tornado candidates. 'In Chasm' is a position/state, not a tier. Not main thesis.

gorilla.name: Gorilla
gorilla.desc: Controls the proprietary architecture that becomes the market standard. Competitors must conform to its interfaces. Switching costs are prohibitively high. Investors should buy and hold indefinitely.

potentialGorilla.name: Potential Gorilla
potentialGorilla.desc: Competing in a tornado where the standard is not yet decided. Has the technical and market momentum to become the gorilla. Buy now before the market recognizes the winner.

king.name: King
king.desc: Market leader in an open category without a single proprietary architecture. Has scale advantages but no architecture lock-in, so competitors can credibly challenge. Generates steady returns but won't compound like a gorilla. Hold lightly.

prince.name: Prince
prince.desc: Credible #2 challenger to the King in an open market. Threatens the King's position but hasn't overtaken it yet. Hold even more lightly than Kings — sell first on any market stumble.

serf.name: Serf
serf.desc: Bottom-tier participant in an open market. Has no differentiated advantage over the King or Prince, and lacks the scale to compete. Cannot generate sustainable returns. Avoid.

chimpanzee.name: Chimpanzee
chimpanzee.desc: Competed for gorilla status but lost the architecture war. Survives in niche segments with a proprietary approach but cannot expand into the mainstream. Sell and redeploy into gorillas or potential gorillas.

monkey.name: Monkey
monkey.desc: Clones the gorilla architecture at a discount price. Has no sustainable moat — the gorilla can reclaim their customers at will. Do not hold for the long term.

inChasm.name: In Chasm
inChasm.desc: Has early adopter enthusiasm but has not crossed to the pragmatist mainstream. Most companies in this position never cross. High risk, avoid until clear beachhead emerges.

learn.actionSubtitle: Core investment actions from Chapter 1 of The Gorilla Game. These tell you what to do at each market phase.
learn.action1Desc: It doesn't matter if you're 3-4 quarters late. The Gorilla Game gets easier over time because you're learning what to look for, not reacting to quarterly signals.
learn.action2Title: Buy a basket of all gorilla candidates
learn.action2Desc: Buy shares in every company that looks like a potential gorilla — as a basket, not individual picks. You can still play the game favorably even 1-2 quarters late.
learn.action3Title: When the gorilla emerges, sell the rest and concentrate
learn.action3Desc: Once the gorilla appears, sell all other stocks in the basket and consolidate into the gorilla. Concentration, not diversification, reduces risk.
learn.action4Title: Plan to hold the gorilla long-term
learn.action4Desc: Take a 'buy and hold' approach. Fewer decisions mean fewer mistakes. Trying to time short-term trades is like rolling dice.
learn.action5Title: Only sell when a substitute technology threatens the category
learn.action5Desc: Gorilla power comes from within the market category. Threats come from outside. This plays out slowly, so you have plenty of time to exit safely.
learn.rulesTitle: ⑦ The 10 Gorilla Game Investment Principles
learn.rulesSubtitle: The core investment operating rules from Geoffrey Moore's The Gorilla Game. If the classification system tells you 'what,' these principles tell you 'when and how.'
learn.rule1Title: Application software: buy in the Bowling Alley
learn.rule1Desc: If the category is application software, begin buying in the Bowling Alley stage — where niche-by-niche adoption signals a gorilla candidate is forming.
learn.rule1En: If the category is application software, begin buying in the bowling alley.
learn.rule2Title: Enabling technology: buy after the Tornado forms
learn.rule2Desc: If the category is enabling hardware or enabling software, wait until the tornado has clearly formed before buying. These categories need mass-market validation.
learn.rule2En: If the category is enabling hardware or software, begin buying after the tornado has formed.
learn.rule3Title: Buy a basket of gorilla candidates
learn.rule3Desc: Buy all gorilla candidates in a basket — usually at least 2, sometimes 3, and normally no more than 4 companies. This hedges against picking the wrong winner.
learn.rule3En: Buy a basket comprising all the gorilla candidates — usually at least two, sometimes three, and normally no more than four companies.
learn.rule4Title: Hold gorilla stocks for the long term
learn.rule4Desc: Hold gorilla stocks indefinitely. Sell only when a proven substitution threat emerges — a discontinuous innovation creating an entirely new market.
learn.rule4En: Hold gorilla stocks for the long term. Sell only on proven substitution threat.
learn.rule5Desc: Hold application software chimp stocks as long as they show potential for further market expansion. Never hold enabling-technology chimps — they have no path to recovery.
learn.rule5En: Hold application software chimp stocks as long as they exhibit potential for further market expansion. Do not hold enabling-technology chimps.
learn.rule6Desc: Hold kings and princes with light positions, selling individual stocks on marketplace stumbles and the entire category upon deceleration of hypergrowth.
learn.rule6En: Hold kings and princes lightly, selling individual stocks on a marketplace stumble and the category upon deceleration of hypergrowth.
learn.rule7Title: Sell immediately if no gorilla potential
learn.rule7Desc: Once it becomes clear that a company will never become a gorilla, sell its stock immediately. Don't hold hoping for a turnaround.
learn.rule7En: Once it becomes clear to you that a company will never become a gorilla, sell its stock.
learn.rule8Desc: Money from sold non-gorilla stocks should immediately be reinvested into remaining gorilla candidates. Capital should always be working toward gorilla exposure.
learn.rule8En: Money should immediately be taken out of non-gorilla stocks and be reinvested in the remaining gorilla candidates.
learn.rule9Title: In a gorilla collision, hold until outcome
learn.rule9Desc: When two gorilla candidates collide head-on, hold your positions in both until there is a definitive outcome. Do not try to pick the winner prematurely.
learn.rule9En: In a gorilla collision, hold your gorilla candidates until there has been a definitive outcome.
learn.rule10Desc: Most news has nothing to do with the gorilla game. Ignore it. Use AI to track pattern changes only from key influencers, specialist publications, and expert analysts.
learn.rule10En: Most news has nothing to do with the gorilla game. Learn to ignore it.
learn.funnelSubtitle: The tool that turns theory into execution. The CIA-GI model adapts Miller & Heiman's Sales Funnel to the investment framework, determining what to focus on at each stage.

prospecting.title: Habitat Scouting (Prospecting)
prospecting.desc: Scan technology categories and markets where gorillas may emerge. Identify markets in the Bowling Alley to early Tornado stages of the TALC.

qualifying.title: Basket Formation (Qualifying)
qualifying.desc: Select 2-4 gorilla candidates within the identified category. Verify architecture control, switching costs, and ecosystem partnerships.

inTheFunnel.title: Tracking & Observation (In the Funnel)
inTheFunnel.desc: Track role changes across basket companies: Gorilla/Chimp/Monkey/King. Use AI to monitor influencers, specialist publications, and analyst communications.

bestFew.title: Gorilla Concentration (Best Few)
bestFew.desc: When the winner is confirmed, sell non-gorilla stocks and concentrate into the confirmed gorilla. Cross-reference TALC phase and Hype Cycle for timing.

learn.qualSheetSubtitle: A living document adapting Miller & Heiman's Blue Sheet to investment analysis. Created per company and updated as market phases progress.

objective.title: Investment Objective
objective.desc: Define your investment goal in one sentence: which company, how much, at what timing, under what conditions.

buyingInfluences.title: Influence Analysis (Buying Influences)
buyingInfluences.desc: Identify 4 forces determining market standards: Economic (budget authority), User (actual users), Technical (spec reviewers), Coach (internal intelligence sources).

responseModes.title: Response Modes
responseModes.desc: Market participant states: Growth (embracing change), Trouble (urgent change needed), Even Keel (resisting change), Overconfident (believes nothing needs to change).

redFlags.title: Red Flags & Strengths
redFlags.desc: Risk factors that could kill the investment (unconfirmed data, substitution threats) and competitive advantages (architecture lock-in, NRR metrics).

winResults.title: Win-Results
winResults.desc: Beyond investment returns, define the gorilla company's win conditions: standard establishment, ecosystem expansion, compounding switching costs.

actionPlan.title: Action Plan
actionPlan.desc: Concrete 'who, when, what' plans to eliminate red flags and leverage strengths. Includes strategy revision triggers when TALC phase changes.

learn.qualSheetNote: The Gorilla Qualification Sheet is not a one-time document — it must be updated with new information as market phases progress, revising strategy as a 'living document.' Collective Intelligence and Active Participation from all members is essential.

crossingTheChasm.title: Crossing the Chasm
crossingTheChasm.subtitle: 3rd Edition (2014)
crossingTheChasm.author: Geoffrey A. Moore
crossingTheChasm.keyIdea: The critical gap between early adopters and the pragmatist early majority. Most tech products die here. The solution: pick one beachhead niche, build a whole product for it, and use that win as a reference to cross into the mainstream.
crossingTheChasm.concepts: Technology Adoption Life Cycle, The Chasm, Whole Product, Beachhead Strategy, D-Day Analogy

insideTheTornado.title: Inside the Tornado
insideTheTornado.author: Geoffrey A. Moore
insideTheTornado.keyIdea: What happens after you cross the chasm. The Bowling Alley (niche adoption), the Tornado (explosive mainstream growth), and Main Street (mature market) each require completely different strategies. In the tornado: ship fast, attack competitors, expand channels.
insideTheTornado.concepts: Bowling Alley, Tornado, Main Street, Pragmatist Herd Behavior, +1 Marketing

theGorillaGame.title: The Gorilla Game
theGorillaGame.author: Geoffrey A. Moore, Paul Johnson, Tom Kippola
theGorillaGame.keyIdea: The investment thesis derived from the tornado framework. In every tornado, one company emerges as the gorilla — the architecture standard-setter with compounding competitive advantage. Buy gorillas and potential gorillas. Sell everything else.
theGorillaGame.concepts: Gorilla / Chimp / Monkey, King / Prince / Serf, Competitive Advantage Period (CAP), Architecture Lock-in, Switching Costs

strategicSelling.title: The New Strategic Selling
strategicSelling.subtitle: Revised & Updated
strategicSelling.author: Robert B. Miller, Stephen E. Heiman
strategicSelling.keyIdea: How enterprise companies win complex B2B deals by identifying all buying influences — Economic Buyer, User Buyers, Technical Buyers, and a Coach. Gorillas dominate not just through technology but through superior coverage of every decision-maker in the customer organization.
strategicSelling.concepts: Complex Sale, Buying Influences, Economic Buyer, Response Modes, Win-Results, Sales Funnel, Red Flags

ciagiAdaptation.title: CIA-GI Strategic Selling Adaptation
ciagiAdaptation.subtitle: 2026 Draft
ciagiAdaptation.author: CIA-GI Working Group
ciagiAdaptation.keyIdea: Adapts Miller & Heiman's Sales Funnel and Blue Sheet methodology to the Gorilla Game investment framework. Transforms the 4-stage sales pipeline into an investment qualification process: from habitat scouting, through basket formation, to gorilla tracking and concentrated positioning. Introduces the Gorilla Qualification Sheet — a living document that maps TALC phase, Hype Cycle position, competitive dynamics, and the 10 Gorilla Game Principles into a single strategic view. Leverages Collective Intelligence and AI-driven signal tracking to minimize manual overhead.
ciagiAdaptation.concepts: Gorilla Investment Funnel, Gorilla Qualification Sheet, Blue Sheet Adaptation, Hype Cycle Integration, 10 Investment Principles, Collective Intelligence, AI Signal Tracking

Gorilla.label: 🦍 Gorilla
Gorilla.desc: Dominates the proprietary architecture that became the market standard. Competitors must conform to its interface. Switching costs are extreme. Buy and hold indefinitely.

Potential Gorilla.label: 🦍 Potential Gorilla
Potential Gorilla.desc: Competing in a tornado where the standard is not yet decided. Has the technical and market momentum to become the gorilla. Buy before the market recognizes the winner.

King.label: 👑 King
King.desc: Market leader in an open market without architecture lock-in. Has scale advantages but competitors can challenge. Hold lightly.

Prince.label: 🤴 Prince
Prince.desc: Credible #2 challenger to the King. Threatening the King's position but hasn't overtaken yet. Hold even more lightly — sell first in downturns.

Serf.label: ⛏️ Serf
Serf.desc: Bottom-tier player in an open market. No differentiation from King or Prince, insufficient scale. Cannot sustain competitive returns. Avoid.

Chimpanzee.label: 🐵 Chimpanzee
Chimpanzee.desc: Competed for gorilla status but lost the architecture war. Survives in niches with proprietary approach but cannot expand to mainstream. Sell and reinvest in gorillas.

Monkey.label: 🐒 Monkey
Monkey.desc: Clones the gorilla's architecture at a discount. No sustainable moat — the gorilla can reclaim its customers at any time. Do not hold long-term.

In Chasm.label: 🕳️ In Chasm
In Chasm.desc: Has early adopter enthusiasm but hasn't crossed to the pragmatist mainstream. Most companies here never cross. High risk — avoid until a clear beachhead emerges.

talcChart.phasesSubtitle: Where each firm sits on the Technology Adoption Life Cycle. The Tornado is the buy window.

marketSummary.footer: Based on Geoffrey Moore's Gorilla Game framework · 50 US tech firms

pipeline.title: Investment Pipeline
pipeline.subtitle: Four investment decision stages based on the Gorilla Game framework. Click any firm for full analysis.
pipeline.buyDesc: Firms that own or are competing for the proprietary architecture standard. Optimal buy window is around the Tornado. Hold long-term.
pipeline.watchDesc: Open market leaders and challengers. No architecture lock-in, so competitive dynamics are fluid. Hold lightly, exit on stumbles.
pipeline.exitDesc: Architecture war losers, discount clones, and undifferentiated open-market players. Sell and redeploy into gorillas.
pipeline.avoidDesc: Firms that haven't reached mainstream adoption. Most never cross the chasm. Do not invest until a clear beachhead emerges.

narratives.Gorilla: {name} is classified as a **Gorilla** — the dominant force in its technology category. With an estimated {sharePercent}% niche market share and strong architecture control (score: {architectureControl}/100), it defines the standard that competitors must conform to. Geoffrey Moore's framework identifies gorillas as the primary investment target: their switching costs are prohibitively high (Net Revenue Retention: {nrr}%), and they are the last company standing after the tornado. Buy and hold.
narratives.Potential Gorilla: {name} is a **Potential Gorilla** — currently competing in a tornado-phase market where the dominant standard is still being decided. With {sharePercent}% niche share and {growthPercent}% revenue growth, it has a credible path to gorilla status. Per Moore's framework, this is the optimal entry point before market consolidation locks in the winner. The risk: another competitor could take the architecture standard instead.
narratives.King: {name} is classified as a **King** — a market leader in an open category without a single dominant proprietary standard. Kings enjoy scale advantages and brand recognition (NRR: {nrr}%) but lack architecture lock-in, so Princes and new challengers can always pursue them. Moore's framework treats Kings as solid but not exceptional investments: they generate steady returns but won't compound like Gorillas. Hold lightly.
narratives.Prince: {name} is classified as a **Prince** — a credible #2 challenger to the King in an open market. With {sharePercent}% niche share and {growthPercent}% revenue growth, it threatens the King's position but hasn't overtaken it yet. In a market without proprietary architecture, switching costs are low and competitive dynamics are fluid. Hold even more lightly than Kings — sell first on any market stumble.
narratives.Serf: {name} is classified as a **Serf** — a bottom-tier participant in an open market. It lacks both the scale of the King and any differentiated architecture (score: {architectureControl}/100). In markets without a proprietary standard, participants without scale cannot build sustainable competitive advantage. Avoid.
narratives.Chimpanzee: {name} is classified as a **Chimpanzee** — a company that competed for gorilla status but did not win the architecture standard. It survives in niche segments where its proprietary approach retains loyal customers, but it cannot expand aggressively into the mainstream. Per Moore's framework, chimpanzees face a shrinking addressable market as gorilla standards absorb more of the customer base. This is a sell signal for long-term investors.
narratives.Monkey: {name} is classified as a **Monkey** — a commodity clone that competes on price against the gorilla's architecture without offering architectural differentiation. Monkeys generate revenue during tornado phases by undercutting the gorilla, but have no sustainable moat. The gorilla can reclaim their customers at will. Moore's framework recommends avoiding monkey stocks for long-term portfolios.
narratives.In Chasm: {name} is classified as **In Chasm** — it has captured early adopter enthusiasm but has not yet crossed to the pragmatist early majority. This is the most dangerous stage of the Technology Adoption Life Cycle: many companies never cross. Until {name} demonstrates a clear beachhead niche domination and a whole product strategy for pragmatists, the investment thesis remains unproven. Avoid.
